name: Statistics
blurb: The stats an analyst actually uses — and the traps.

lessons.title: Mean, Median & Percentiles: Which 'Average' Is Lying to You?
lessons.concept:
Most people say 'average' and mean the arithmetic mean: add everything up, divide by how many. But the mean has a fatal weakness — it gets dragged toward extreme values. Imagine a small startup in Gurugram with 9 employees earning ₹6 lakh/year each and 1 founder-CEO drawing ₹3 crore. The mean salary is (9×6 + 300)/10 = ₹35.4 lakh. Technically true, wildly misleading — nobody there earns anything close to ₹35 lakh except one person. The median — the middle value when you line everyone up — is ₹6 lakh, which honestly describes a typical employee. Whenever data is skewed (incomes, house prices, page-load times, order values), the median is usually the more truthful summary.

Percentiles generalise the median. The median is just the 50th percentile: 50% of values fall below it. The 90th percentile (P90) is the value below which 90% of your data sits. These are the backbone of real-world monitoring. When an engineering team says 'our P95 API latency is 400ms', they mean 95% of requests were faster than 400ms and the slowest 5% were worse — and it's precisely that slow 5% that annoys users. A mean latency hides this completely, because a few very fast requests can mask a painful tail.

The practical rule: use the mean when data is roughly symmetric and you need a value you can add up or do arithmetic with (like computing total revenue from average order value × orders). Use the median and percentiles when data is skewed or when you care about the typical case and the tail separately. A good analyst almost never reports a single number — they report the median AND a couple of percentiles, so the shape of reality survives the summary.
lessons.example: Zomato/Swiggy delivery times in a city: most orders arrive in 25-35 minutes, but a few during heavy rain take 90+ minutes. The mean delivery time might be 38 minutes, but the median is 30. If the company promises 'average 38 min' it sounds worse than reality for most customers; reporting 'median 30 min, P90 55 min' tells both the typical and the worst-realistic story honestly.
lessons.code:
# Python: mean vs median vs percentiles on skewed salary data
import numpy as np
salaries = [6, 6, 6, 6, 6, 6, 6, 6, 6, 300]  # in lakhs; last is the CEO

print('Mean  :', np.mean(salaries))          # 35.4  <- dragged up by CEO
print('Median:', np.median(salaries))        # 6.0   <- typical employee
print('P90   :', np.percentile(salaries, 90))# 33.6
print('P50   :', np.percentile(salaries, 50))# 6.0  (same as median)
lessons.tryit: Take the monthly rent of 8-10 flats in your locality (check NoBroker or ask neighbours). Compute the mean and the median by hand. If they differ by more than ~15%, your data is skewed — write one sentence on which number you'd quote to a friend moving in, and why.

mistakes.m: Reporting the mean for skewed data like income, house prices, or wait times, then being surprised it doesn't match anyone's experience.
mistakes.fix: When min, median, and max are far apart (right-skewed), lead with the median. Show the mean only alongside it, never alone.

mistakes.m: Thinking 'P90 = 90% chance' or that percentiles are probabilities.
mistakes.fix: P90 is a value on the same scale as your data (e.g. 55 minutes). It means 90% of observations are at or below it — it is a position in the sorted data, not a chance.

mistakes.m: Confusing 'average person is above average' being impossible — but with the median, more than half CAN be on one side of the mean.
mistakes.fix: In right-skewed data most people fall below the mean. That's normal and expected, not a paradox; it's exactly why median is more representative.

links.t: Khan Academy: Mean, median, and mode

quiz.q: A dataset of 100 order values has mean ₹1,200 and median ₹700. What does this most likely tell you?
opts: The data has an error
The data is right-skewed with some large orders pulling the mean up
Half the orders are worth exactly ₹700
The mean is always the better summary here
quiz.why: When the mean sits well above the median, a minority of large values (big orders) are dragging the mean up while most orders cluster lower. This is the classic signature of right-skew, and it's why the median (₹700) better describes a typical order.
quiz.approach: Compare mean vs median first: mean >> median signals right-skew, mean << median signals left-skew. Let the gap tell you the shape before you summarise.

quiz.q: An SRE says 'P95 latency is 300ms'. What is the correct interpretation?
opts: 95% of requests were slower than 300ms
The average request took 300ms
5% of requests were slower than 300ms
There is a 95% chance the next request takes 300ms
quiz.why: P95 is the value below which 95% of requests fall, so the remaining 5% — the slow tail — were slower than 300ms. Percentiles describe positions in sorted data, not the mean and not a probability for a single future request.
quiz.approach: For latency and user experience, watch the high percentiles (P95/P99). The painful minority lives in the tail, exactly where the mean hides it.

quiz.q: For which quantity is the arithmetic mean clearly the RIGHT choice?
opts: Typical household income in a city
Computing total revenue as average order value × number of orders
Describing a typical API response time
Summarising house prices in an area
quiz.why: The mean is designed to be additive: mean × count reconstructs the total exactly. So average order value × orders gives true total revenue. The other three are skewed quantities where the median describes the typical case more honestly.
quiz.approach: Use the mean when you need arithmetic you can add back up (totals, rates). Use the median for 'what's typical' in skewed data.

lessons.title: Correlation vs Causation: Why Ice Cream Doesn't Cause Drowning
lessons.concept:
Two things moving together is called correlation. Correlation is measured from -1 to +1: +1 means they rise together in perfect lockstep, -1 means one rises exactly as the other falls, and 0 means no linear relationship. Ice-cream sales and drowning deaths are strongly positively correlated across the year — but ice cream doesn't drown anyone. A third factor, summer heat, drives both: hot days mean more ice cream AND more people swimming. This hidden third factor is called a confounder, and it is the number-one reason correlation is not causation.

Causation means changing one thing actually makes the other change. The gold standard for proving it is a randomised experiment (which you'll meet in the A/B testing lesson): if you randomly assign who gets the 'cause' and the outcome still differs, you've isolated a real effect. Without randomisation, you're left with observational data where confounders lurk everywhere. A famous Indian-context example: cities with more English-medium schools have higher average incomes. Does English education cause wealth? Partly maybe — but richer families and richer cities can also afford more such schools, so wealth causes the schools too. Reverse causation and confounding are both live possibilities.

There's a second trap even when correlation is real and strong: it might be a coincidence, especially when you go fishing through many variables. Look at enough pairs of unrelated time series — nCr grows fast — and some will correlate at 0.9 purely by chance. This is why analysts distrust a correlation found by trawling data with no prior reason to expect it. The discipline is: state a plausible mechanism BEFORE you look, be suspicious of confounders, and reserve the word 'causes' for evidence from experiments or very carefully controlled analysis.
lessons.example: An e-commerce team notices that customers who use the wishlist feature spend 3x more. Tempting conclusion: 'push everyone to use wishlists to boost spending!' But engaged, high-intent buyers are both more likely to use wishlists AND to spend more — buying intent is the confounder. Forcing a casual browser to add a wishlist item won't magically make them a big spender. Only an A/B test (randomly nudging some users to the wishlist) can reveal whether the feature itself causes extra spend.
lessons.code:
# Python: strong correlation, zero causation (both driven by 'heat')
import numpy as np
heat = np.array([20, 25, 30, 35, 40, 38, 33, 28])   # confounder: temperature
icecream = heat * 100 + np.random.normal(0, 50, 8)   # driven by heat
drownings = heat * 0.5 + np.random.normal(0, 1, 8)   # also driven by heat

r = np.corrcoef(icecream, drownings)[0, 1]
print('Correlation icecream vs drownings:', round(r, 2))  # ~0.9, but NO causal link
lessons.tryit: Find a 'spurious correlations' example online (or invent one from your own life: your coffee intake vs your manager's email count). Write down (1) the correlation you'd expect, (2) at least one plausible confounder, and (3) what experiment could test real causation.

mistakes.m: Seeing a strong correlation and immediately writing 'X drives Y' in a report or recommendation.
mistakes.fix: Ask three questions first: Could Y cause X (reverse)? Could a third factor Z cause both (confounder)? Could this be coincidence from data-fishing? Only after ruling these out, and ideally an experiment, use causal language.

mistakes.m: Believing correlation near 0 means 'no relationship at all'.
mistakes.fix: Correlation measures only LINEAR association. A U-shaped relationship (e.g. productivity vs hours worked) can have correlation near 0 while being strongly related. Always plot the data.

mistakes.m: Trusting a correlation found by testing dozens of variable pairs with no prior hypothesis.
mistakes.fix: Test enough pairs and chance alone produces strong correlations. Form the hypothesis first, then check it — don't let the data mine you.

links.t: Wikipedia: Correlation does not imply causation

quiz.q: A study finds cities with more police officers have more reported crimes. The best interpretation is:
opts: Police cause crime
A confounder like city population likely drives both
Reducing police will reduce crime
Correlation here proves causation
quiz.why: Bigger cities have both more police AND more crime simply because they have more people. Population is a confounder driving both variables, so the raw correlation says nothing about police causing crime. You would need to compare like-for-like or run an experiment.
quiz.approach: When two things correlate, always hunt for a size/scale confounder (population, revenue, traffic) before drawing any causal conclusion.

quiz.q: A correlation coefficient of 0 between two variables means:
opts: The variables are unrelated in every way
There is no LINEAR relationship, but there could be a curved one
One variable causes the other to stay constant
The data is wrong
quiz.why: The standard correlation coefficient only captures linear (straight-line) association. Variables can have a strong non-linear (e.g. U-shaped) relationship and still show correlation near 0. Plotting the data is the only way to see such patterns.
quiz.approach: Never conclude 'no relationship' from a low correlation alone — always look at the scatter plot to catch non-linear patterns.

quiz.q: What is the single strongest way to establish that X actually causes Y?
opts: Find a very high correlation, above 0.95
Randomly assign X in a controlled experiment and observe Y
Collect more observational data
Check that X happens before Y in time
quiz.why: Randomly assigning who gets X breaks the link between X and any confounders, so a resulting difference in Y can be attributed to X itself. High correlation, more data, and time-ordering all help build a case but none of them rules out confounders the way randomisation does.
quiz.approach: When you truly need causation, reach for a randomised experiment (A/B test). Observational correlations, however strong, only generate hypotheses.

lessons.title: Sampling & Bias: How 500 People Can Speak for a Billion
lessons.concept:
You almost never measure everyone. Exit polls predict a national election from a few lakh voters; a factory tests the strength of a batch by breaking a handful of samples. This works because of a beautiful fact: a well-drawn random sample of even a few hundred to a few thousand people can estimate a population of a billion surprisingly accurately. The key word is RANDOM — every member of the population must have a known, non-zero chance of being picked. When that holds, the maths guarantees your estimate closes in on the truth, and — counter-intuitively — the accuracy depends mostly on the sample SIZE, not on what fraction of the population you sampled. 2,000 randomly chosen Indians tell you almost as much about India as 2,000 randomly chosen Maldivians tell you about the Maldives.

The destroyer of all this is bias: a systematic error that no amount of extra sample size can fix. If you survey people about smartphone usage by messaging them on WhatsApp, you've already excluded everyone without a smartphone — this is selection bias. If you poll voters only in urban malls, you miss rural India entirely. A classic real trap is survivorship bias: analysing only the startups that succeeded to find 'the secret of success', while the graveyard of identical-strategy failures is invisible. And non-response bias bites hard: if only angry customers bother to fill your feedback form, your ratings look far worse than reality.

Here is the crucial mental model: sampling error (random noise from picking a subset) shrinks as your sample grows, and you can measure it. Bias (systematic tilt in HOW you sampled) does not shrink with size — a biased survey of 10 lakh people is still wrong, just confidently wrong. So a good analyst spends more worry on 'who might I be systematically missing?' than on 'is my sample big enough?'. The most famous cautionary tale is the 1936 US Literary Digest poll: 2.4 million responses, and it still called the election wrong, because its sample (car and phone owners in the Depression) was systematically richer than the electorate.
lessons.example: A food-delivery app runs an in-app survey: 'Rate your experience'. It concludes customers are unhappy (average 2.8/5) and panics. But in-app surveys are answered mostly by people who had a problem — happy customers just eat and close the app. This is non-response/self-selection bias. A better read comes from randomly calling a sample of ALL recent customers, where the real average turns out to be 4.1/5.
lessons.code:
# Python: a bigger sample cuts random error, but NOT bias
import numpy as np
np.random.seed(1)
true_mean = 4.1  # true satisfaction across ALL customers

# Unbiased random sample: estimate is close to truth
fair = np.random.normal(true_mean, 1.0, 2000)
print('Fair random sample mean :', round(fair.mean(), 2))  # ~4.1

# Biased sample: only unhappy people respond (systematic tilt down by 1.3)
biased = np.random.normal(true_mean - 1.3, 1.0, 200000)
print('Biased (huge) sample mean:', round(biased.mean(), 2)) # ~2.8, still WRONG
lessons.tryit: Design a 3-question survey to estimate the average daily commute time of employees in your company. Write down exactly HOW you'll pick who to ask — then list two groups your method might systematically miss (e.g. work-from-home staff, night-shift workers) and how you'd include them.

mistakes.m: Believing a huge sample size automatically means a trustworthy result.
mistakes.fix: Size only reduces random error. If the sampling method is biased, a million responses stay wrong. Interrogate HOW people entered the sample before trusting the number.

mistakes.m: Drawing lessons only from winners (successful funds, viral posts, unicorn startups).
mistakes.fix: That's survivorship bias. Always ask 'where are the failures that did the same thing?' The invisible losers usually break the pattern you spotted.

mistakes.m: Treating a convenience sample (whoever was easy to reach — your Twitter followers, mall shoppers) as if it represents everyone.
mistakes.fix: Convenience samples are systematically skewed toward the reachable group. State the limitation explicitly, or use random selection from the full population list.

links.t: Khan Academy: Sampling methods and bias

quiz.q: An online poll on a news website gets 5 lakh responses and predicts a landslide. Why should you be cautious?
opts: 5 lakh is too small a sample
People who visit that site and choose to vote may not represent all voters — self-selection bias
Online polls are always exactly right
You need at least 1 crore responses
quiz.why: The respondents self-selected by visiting that particular site and choosing to click — they skew toward that site's audience and the politically motivated. This systematic tilt is not fixed by having 5 lakh responses; the sample is large but biased.
quiz.approach: For any poll, ask 'who could enter this sample and who couldn't?' before looking at the size. Self-selection quietly poisons big online samples.

quiz.q: A mutual-fund brochure shows the average return of its currently-running funds is 14%. What bias should you suspect?
opts: Confirmation bias
Survivorship bias — closed/failed funds are excluded
Anchoring bias
No bias, the number is fine
quiz.why: Funds that performed badly get merged or shut down and drop out of the 'currently running' list. Averaging only the survivors inflates the reported return. The true average across all funds ever launched is lower because the failures are missing.
quiz.approach: Whenever you see an average computed over 'the ones still around', mentally add back the dropouts. Survivorship bias inflates almost every winners-only statistic.

quiz.q: Which change most improves the trustworthiness of a customer-satisfaction estimate?
opts: Increase a biased in-app survey from 2,000 to 200,000 responses
Randomly select customers from the full customer list and follow up to reduce non-response
Only survey your most loyal customers
Round the average to one decimal place
quiz.why: Random selection from the full list plus chasing non-responders attacks the actual problem — systematic bias in who answers. Simply scaling up a biased survey keeps the same tilt, and surveying only loyal customers makes the bias worse.
quiz.approach: Fix the sampling frame and non-response before chasing bigger numbers. Representativeness beats raw volume every time.

lessons.title: A/B Testing & p<0.05: Proving a Change Actually Worked
lessons.concept:
An A/B test is a randomised experiment for the digital world. You want to know if a new checkout button (version B) beats the old one (version A). You randomly split incoming users: half see A, half see B. Randomisation is the magic ingredient — it makes the two groups statistically identical in every way (device, city, intent, time of day) EXCEPT the button, so any difference in conversion can be credited to the button itself. This is how you leap from correlation to causation. Never assign by choice or convenience (e.g. Android users get B) — that reintroduces confounders and destroys the whole point.

Suppose B converts at 12% and A at 10%. Is B really better, or did B just get luckier users this week? Enter the p-value. It answers one precise question: 'If there were truly NO difference between A and B, how likely is it we'd see a gap this big (or bigger) just from random chance?' A small p-value means 'this gap would be a weird fluke if the button did nothing', so you conclude the button probably does something. The convention p < 0.05 means: less than a 5% chance of seeing this result if there were no real effect. Crucially, p < 0.05 does NOT mean '95% chance B is better' and it says nothing about how BIG or how valuable the effect is — a tiny 0.1% lift can be 'statistically significant' with millions of users yet be commercially worthless.

Two disciplines protect you. First, sample size: you must decide in advance how many users you need, because tiny tests are dominated by noise and can't detect real effects. More users mean you can detect smaller true differences reliably. Second — and this is where most teams cheat without realising — do NOT peek and stop early the moment p dips below 0.05. If you keep checking a running test and stop at the first 'significant' moment, random noise will eventually cross 0.05 by chance even when nothing is happening, giving you a false winner. Decide your sample size and test duration up front, let it run to completion, and only then read the result. Run at least one full business cycle (usually a week) so weekday/weekend behaviour is included.
lessons.example: A fintech app tests a new one-tap UPI flow (B) against the old two-step flow (A). They randomly route 50,000 users to each. After a full week: A converts at 68%, B at 71%, p = 0.01. Because they fixed the sample size in advance and didn't stop early, they can trust the 3-point lift is real, not noise — and 3 points on lakhs of daily transactions is hugely valuable. Had they peeked daily and stopped on day 2 when p first hit 0.04, they'd risk shipping a fluke.
lessons.code:
# Python: two-proportion A/B test with a pre-decided sample
from scipy import stats
import numpy as np

# A: 5000 users, 500 conversions (10%).  B: 5000 users, 600 conversions (12%)
conv_A, n_A = 500, 5000
conv_B, n_B = 600, 5000

# z-test for two proportions
p_pool = (conv_A + conv_B) / (n_A + n_B)
se = np.sqrt(p_pool*(1-p_pool)*(1/n_A + 1/n_B))
z  = (conv_B/n_B - conv_A/n_A) / se
p_value = 2 * (1 - stats.norm.cdf(abs(z)))
print('Lift:', conv_B/n_B - conv_A/n_A)   # 0.02 (2 percentage points)
print('p-value:', round(p_value, 4))       # ~0.0016  -> significant at p<0.05
lessons.tryit: Design an A/B test for a real change you'd like to make (e.g. a new email subject line). Write down: (1) the ONE metric you'll judge on, (2) how you'll randomly split users, (3) roughly how long you'll run it, and (4) the promise to yourself that you will NOT stop early when it first looks good.

mistakes.m: Reading 'p < 0.05' as '95% probability that B is better' or as proof the effect is large/important.
mistakes.fix: p < 0.05 only means the observed gap would be unlikely if there were no real difference. It says nothing about the probability B is better, nor about effect size. Always report the actual lift and a confidence interval alongside p.

mistakes.m: Peeking at the dashboard daily and stopping the test the moment p dips below 0.05.
mistakes.fix: Repeated peeking inflates false positives massively — noise will eventually cross 0.05 on its own. Fix sample size and duration up front, and only judge at the end (or use a proper sequential-testing method).

mistakes.m: Running a test on 200 users for one afternoon and declaring a winner.
mistakes.fix: Tiny, short tests are pure noise and can't detect real effects. Estimate the required sample size beforehand and run at least one full business cycle (typically a week) to capture weekday/weekend patterns.

quiz.q: In an A/B test, what is the single most important reason to assign users to A and B randomly?
opts: It makes the test run faster
It makes the two groups statistically identical except for the change, allowing a causal conclusion
It guarantees exactly equal group sizes
It removes the need for a p-value
quiz.why: Randomisation balances all other factors — device, location, intent, time — across the two groups on average, so the only systematic difference left is the change you made. That's what lets you attribute any outcome gap to the change itself rather than a confounder.
quiz.approach: Randomise assignment always. The moment you split by any user attribute instead, confounders creep back and you lose the ability to claim causation.

quiz.q: A test yields p = 0.03. Which statement is correct?
opts: There is a 97% chance version B is better
If there were truly no difference, there's only a 3% chance of seeing a gap this large or larger
The effect is definitely large and worth shipping
B is 3% better than A
quiz.why: The p-value is computed under the assumption of NO real difference; p = 0.03 means such an extreme result would occur only 3% of the time by chance in that no-effect world. It is not the probability B is better, not a statement about effect size, and not the lift itself.
quiz.approach: State p-values in the conditional form 'if there were no effect, this result would be rare'. Then separately report the actual lift to judge whether it's worth shipping.

quiz.q: Your team checks the A/B dashboard every day and plans to stop as soon as p < 0.05. What's the danger?
opts: The test will run too slowly
Repeated peeking inflates the false-positive rate, so you may declare a winner that's just noise
The p-value can never go below 0.05 this way
Nothing — stopping early is best practice
quiz.why: Each peek is another chance for random fluctuation to momentarily cross 0.05. Stopping at the first crossing cherry-picks noise, so even a genuinely useless change will 'win' surprisingly often. Fixing sample size and duration in advance controls this error.
quiz.approach: Decide sample size and end date before launch, and only evaluate at the end (or use a formal sequential test designed for peeking).

lessons.title: Mean, Median, Percentiles: Why the 'Average' Lies About Salaries
lessons.concept:
The mean (arithmetic average) is just total divided by count. It's the balance point of your data — every single value tugs on it. That's its strength and its fatal weakness: one billionaire in a room of 100 people makes the 'average net worth' meaningless. The median is the middle value when you line everyone up in order — the 50th person out of 100. It doesn't care how rich the richest person is; it only cares about who sits in the middle. This is why the median is 'robust' to outliers and the mean is not.

Here is the rule that will save you a hundred times: when a distribution is skewed (has a long tail), the mean gets dragged toward the tail, away from where most people actually are. Indian income is heavily right-skewed — a small number of very high earners pull the mean up. That is exactly why economists report *median* household income, not mean. If someone tells you 'the average salary at our company is ₹18 lakh', ask for the median. If the median is ₹9 lakh, you now know a handful of senior/founder salaries are inflating the mean, and a typical employee earns closer to ₹9 lakh.

Percentiles generalise the median. The p-th percentile is the value below which p% of the data falls. The median is just the 50th percentile. Percentiles are the honest way to describe spread: the 90th percentile (p90) salary tells you what the top earners make; the 10th percentile (p10) tells you the floor. In engineering and product, p95 and p99 latency matter far more than the mean — a mean API response of 200ms can hide the reality that 1% of your users wait 4 seconds. The mean averages the pain away; percentiles show you who's actually hurting.
lessons.example: A 10-person startup in Gurugram pays (in ₹ lakh/year): 6, 7, 7, 8, 8, 9, 10, 12, 15, and the founder takes 120. The mean salary is (6+7+7+8+8+9+10+12+15+120)/10 = ₹20.2 lakh. But nine of ten employees earn *below* that 'average'. The median — average of the 5th and 6th values (8 and 9) — is ₹8.5 lakh, which honestly describes a typical employee. When a recruiter quotes an 'average CTC', this gap is exactly the trick to watch for.
lessons.code:
# Python: mean vs median vs percentiles on skewed salary data
import numpy as np
salaries = np.array([6,7,7,8,8,9,10,12,15,120])  # lakh/year

print('Mean  :', salaries.mean())          # 20.2  <- dragged up by 120
print('Median:', np.median(salaries))      # 8.5   <- the typical person
print('p90   :', np.percentile(salaries, 90))  # top-earner floor
print('p10   :', np.percentile(salaries, 10))  # bottom floor

# Rule of thumb: mean >> median  =>  right-skew, report the median
lessons.tryit: Take your own team's or department's monthly salaries (or any 10+ numbers with one big outlier). Compute the mean and the median in Excel with =AVERAGE() and =MEDIAN(). Note the gap, then compute =PERCENTILE.INC(range, 0.9). Write one sentence explaining which number you'd quote to a new hire and why.

mistakes.m: Treating the mean as 'the typical value' regardless of the data's shape.
mistakes.fix: Check for skew first. If mean and median differ a lot, the data is skewed and the median describes the typical case far better.

mistakes.m: Reporting or optimising the mean latency/response time for a service.
mistakes.fix: Use p95 or p99. The mean hides the tail where your worst user experiences live; percentiles expose them.

mistakes.m: Thinking the median throws away information so it must be less accurate.
mistakes.fix: The median is not less accurate — it answers a different, often more useful question ('who is in the middle?') and is robust to outliers that would distort the mean.

quiz.q: A company's mean salary is ₹22 lakh but its median salary is ₹9 lakh. What does this most likely tell you?
opts: The data is left-skewed and most people earn above ₹22 lakh
The data is right-skewed; a few very high salaries pull the mean up, and ₹9 lakh better reflects a typical employee
Half the employees earn exactly ₹22 lakh
The mean was calculated incorrectly
quiz.why: When the mean sits well above the median, a long right tail (a few very high earners) is dragging the mean upward. The median, being the middle value, is unaffected by those outliers and better describes the typical employee.
quiz.approach: Whenever mean >> median, suspect right-skew and lead with the median for 'typical'.

quiz.q: Your API has a mean response time of 180ms but users complain about slowness. Which single metric best exposes the problem?
opts: The mean response time again, computed more precisely
The mode of response times
The p99 (99th percentile) response time
The total number of requests
quiz.why: The mean averages the painful slow requests together with fast ones, hiding the tail. The p99 shows what the slowest 1% of requests experience — exactly the users who complain — which the mean conceals.
quiz.approach: For latency and user-pain metrics, monitor tail percentiles (p95/p99), not the mean.

quiz.q: Which statement about the median is correct?
opts: The median is always equal to the mean
The median is heavily distorted by a single extreme outlier
The median is the 50th percentile and is robust to outliers
The median can only be computed for normally distributed data
quiz.why: The median is by definition the 50th percentile — the middle value when data is ordered — and because it depends only on rank, not magnitude, one extreme value barely moves it. It applies to any data, skewed or not.
quiz.approach: Reach for the median (and percentiles) whenever outliers or skew are present.

lessons.title: Skew, Distributions, and the Shape Your Data Is Actually In
lessons.concept:
A distribution is simply the shape of your data — how frequently each value occurs. The one everyone knows is the normal (bell) curve: symmetric, with mean = median = mode, most values clustered near the centre. Heights, measurement errors, and many natural quantities are roughly normal. But — and this is the intermediate-level insight — most *business* data is NOT normal. Money, waiting times, city populations, page views, and startup valuations are almost always right-skewed with a long tail on the high side.

Skew tells you which way the tail stretches. In a right-skewed (positively skewed) distribution, the tail points right toward large values, and the order becomes mode < median < mean — the mean sits highest because the big outliers pull it. Indian household income, property prices in Mumbai, and Zomato order values are classic right-skew: most orders are ₹200–500, but a few ₹5,000 party orders stretch the tail. A left-skewed distribution (tail on the low side) is rarer in business — think exam scores on an easy test, where most students score high and a few fail badly, giving mean < median.

Why does shape matter beyond curiosity? Because the shape decides which tools are valid. Averages, standard deviations, and many statistical tests quietly assume symmetry or near-normality. Apply them blindly to skewed data and you'll draw wrong conclusions — you'll over-estimate the 'typical' customer, mis-size inventory, or set SLAs that most users miss. The professional habit: before you compute a single summary statistic, plot a histogram. Ten seconds of looking at the shape prevents hours of wrong analysis. If it's skewed, lead with the median and percentiles, and consider a log transform to tame the tail.
lessons.example: Look at food-delivery order values on Swiggy in a metro. Plot a histogram and you'll see a tall cluster around ₹250–400 (single meals) with a long thin tail stretching to ₹3,000+ (family/party orders). It's textbook right-skew: mean order value might be ₹450 while the median is ₹320. If a category manager plans kitchen capacity around the ₹450 'average', they systematically mis-plan, because no single common order looks like the mean.
lessons.code:
# Python: see the shape before trusting any summary
import numpy as np
# Simulate right-skewed order values (lognormal is a good model for money)
orders = np.random.lognormal(mean=5.7, sigma=0.5, size=10000)

print('Mean  :', round(orders.mean()))       # pulled up by the tail
print('Median:', round(np.median(orders)))    # lower, more typical
# mean > median  =>  right-skew confirmed

# A log transform makes skewed money data roughly symmetric:
log_orders = np.log(orders)   # now mean and median nearly coincide
lessons.tryit: Pull any real numeric column you have — order values, ticket resolution times, page load times — and make a histogram (Excel: Insert > Chart > Histogram, or df.hist() in pandas). Decide from the shape alone whether it's roughly normal, right-skewed, or left-skewed, then check: does mean > median (right) or mean < median (left)?

mistakes.m: Assuming data is normally distributed by default and applying mean ± standard deviation to describe it.
mistakes.fix: Plot a histogram first. Business data (money, time, counts) is usually right-skewed, where mean ± SD gives a misleading picture.

mistakes.m: Confusing skew direction — thinking a long right tail means 'left-skewed'.
mistakes.fix: Skew is named after where the tail points. A tail stretching toward large values is right- (positive-) skewed, even though the hump sits on the left.

mistakes.m: Removing outliers to 'force' data to look normal.
mistakes.fix: In skewed data the tail is often real, not error. Prefer a log transform or robust statistics (median, IQR) instead of deleting genuine high values.

quiz.q: In a right-skewed distribution of customer order values, how do the mean, median, and mode typically order?
opts: mean < median < mode
mode < median < mean
mean = median = mode
median < mode < mean
quiz.why: In a right-skewed distribution the long tail on the high side pulls the mean up the most, the median sits in the middle, and the mode (most common value) is lowest — giving mode < median < mean.
quiz.approach: Use the mean-vs-median gap as a quick skew detector before summarising.

quiz.q: You plot a histogram of API latencies and see a tall spike near 100ms with a long thin tail toward 5000ms. What is the shape?
opts: Left-skewed
Perfectly normal
Right-skewed (positively skewed)
Uniform
quiz.why: The tail stretches toward large (high) values on the right, which is the definition of right/positive skew — even though the bulk of the data (the hump) sits on the left.
quiz.approach: Name skew by where the tail points, not where the hump is.

quiz.q: Why is plotting a histogram before computing summary statistics a good habit?
opts: Histograms are required by law for reporting
The shape reveals skew and outliers, which determine whether the mean/SD are even meaningful
It guarantees the data is normally distributed
It replaces the need for any numeric summary
quiz.why: The distribution's shape decides which summaries are valid: mean and standard deviation assume rough symmetry, so on skewed data they mislead. A quick histogram exposes skew and outliers so you pick the right tools (median, percentiles, log transform).
quiz.approach: Look at the shape first; choose statistics to match the distribution.

lessons.title: Correlation vs Causation, Confounders, and Simpson's Paradox
lessons.concept:
Correlation measures whether two things move together, summarised by the correlation coefficient r, which runs from -1 to +1. r = +1 is a perfect straight-line increase, r = -1 a perfect decrease, r = 0 no linear relationship. Ice-cream sales and drowning deaths are strongly positively correlated in India — both rise in summer. Does ice cream cause drowning? Obviously not. A third variable, temperature (a *confounder*), drives both. This is the single most abused idea in analytics: correlation is necessary but nowhere near sufficient evidence of causation. Two more traps: reverse causation (does stress cause poor sleep, or poor sleep cause stress?) and pure coincidence in large datasets, where some pairs correlate by luck alone. Also remember r only captures *linear* relationships — a strong curved (U-shaped) relationship can have r near 0.

The only reliable way to establish causation is a controlled experiment — randomly assign who gets the 'treatment' so that confounders are, on average, balanced across groups (that's why A/B testing exists, and it's the next lesson). Without randomisation, you're stuck with observational data, where a lurking confounder can always be hiding. The professional move is to ask, every single time you see a correlation: 'What third variable could be driving both?' Nine times out of ten, naming a plausible confounder changes the recommendation.

Simpson's paradox is the confounder problem at its most vicious: a trend that appears in every subgroup can reverse when the groups are combined. The real-world classic is the 1973 UC Berkeley admissions case — overall, men were admitted at a higher rate than women, yet within most individual departments women were admitted at an equal or higher rate. The confounder was that women applied more to competitive departments with low admit rates for everyone. Aggregate the data and the story flips. The lesson: an aggregate number can lie about every group inside it. Always ask whether an important subgroup split changes the direction of the story before you act on a headline number.
lessons.example: A CRED-style product team sees that users who use the 'rewards' feature have far higher retention, and concludes 'rewards cause retention — push everyone to rewards.' But engagement level is a confounder: already-loyal, highly engaged users are the ones who bother to explore rewards. The rewards feature may add little on its own. Only a randomised rollout — give a random half access to rewards, hold the other half back — separates 'rewards cause retention' from 'loyal users happen to use rewards.'
lessons.code:
# Simpson's paradox in numbers: two treatments, overall vs by severity
# Treatment A: mild 81/87 = 93%, severe 192/263 = 73%
#   -> overall 273/350 = 78%
# Treatment B: mild 234/270 = 87%, severe 55/80 = 69%
#   -> overall 289/350 = 83%
#
# B wins OVERALL (83% > 78%), yet A wins in BOTH subgroups
# (93%>87% mild, 73%>69% severe). The confounder is case mix:
# A treated mostly severe cases, B mostly mild ones.
# Moral: always split by the key subgroup before ranking.
lessons.tryit: Find two metrics in your work that move together (e.g., marketing spend and revenue, or feature usage and retention). Write down at least two plausible confounders that could drive both, and one reverse-causation story. Then describe the randomised experiment you'd run to actually test causation.

mistakes.m: Reading a strong correlation as proof that one variable causes the other.
mistakes.fix: Correlation is evidence, not proof. List candidate confounders and reverse-causation stories; only a randomised experiment (or careful causal design) establishes cause.

mistakes.m: Trusting an aggregate rate without checking subgroups.
mistakes.fix: Split by the obvious segments (new vs repeat, city tier, case severity). If the direction flips — Simpson's paradox — the aggregate is misleading you.

mistakes.m: Assuming r near 0 means 'no relationship'.
mistakes.fix: r only measures linear association. Plot the scatter — a clear U-shape or threshold effect can hide behind r ≈ 0.

links.t: Wikipedia: Correlation does not imply causation

quiz.q: Ice-cream sales and drowning deaths rise and fall together across the year. What is the best explanation?
opts: Eating ice cream causes people to drown
Drowning incidents cause more ice-cream sales
A confounder — hot weather — independently increases both
The correlation is a calculation error
quiz.why: Both variables are driven by a third factor, temperature: hot months raise ice-cream sales and also raise swimming (hence drownings). Neither causes the other; the confounder creates the correlation.
quiz.approach: For any correlation, ask 'what third variable could drive both?' before inferring cause.

quiz.q: Treatment A beats Treatment B in both mild and severe patients, yet B has the better overall success rate. What is happening?
opts: The data must be wrong — this is impossible
Simpson's paradox: an unequal case mix reverses the aggregate
B is genuinely better for every patient
The two treatments are statistically identical
quiz.why: This is Simpson's paradox. If A treated mostly hard (severe) cases and B mostly easy (mild) cases, the aggregate is dominated by that mix, so B can lead overall while losing in every subgroup. The subgroup comparison is the trustworthy one here.
quiz.approach: Before ranking on an aggregate, check whether a key subgroup split reverses it.

quiz.q: A scatter plot shows a clear strong U-shaped relationship between two variables, but the correlation coefficient r is about 0. Why?
opts: The variables are truly unrelated
r measures only linear association, so it misses curved relationships
The sample size must be too small
r near 0 always means causation, not correlation
quiz.why: The Pearson correlation coefficient captures only the strength of a straight-line relationship. A symmetric U-shape has strong dependence but no net linear trend, so r can be near 0 despite an obvious pattern.
quiz.approach: Always plot the data; don't rely on r alone to declare 'no relationship'.

lessons.title: A/B Testing, Confidence Intervals, and Base Rates Without Fooling Yourself
lessons.concept:
An A/B test is a mini randomised experiment: split users randomly into control (A) and treatment (B), change one thing, and measure the difference. Randomisation is the magic — it balances confounders on average, so a difference in outcomes can be credited to the change, not to who happened to be in each group. But three things wreck real-world A/B tests. First, sample size: with too few users, random noise easily masquerades as a real effect. You size the test *before* launching, based on the smallest effect worth detecting. Second, the p-value: p < 0.05 conventionally means 'if there were truly no difference, data this extreme (or more) would occur less than 5% of the time.' It is NOT the probability that B is better, and 0.05 is a convention, not a law of nature. Third, peeking: checking results repeatedly and stopping the moment you see p < 0.05 dramatically inflates false positives — every peek is another lottery ticket for a fluke. Fix the sample size, wait, then look once (or use a proper sequential-testing method).

A confidence interval (CI) is the honest companion to any estimate. Instead of claiming 'conversion rose 3%', you say 'conversion rose 3%, 95% CI [0.5%, 5.5%].' The correct interpretation is about the procedure: if you repeated the experiment many times, about 95% of the intervals you construct this way would contain the true value. Practically, a CI shows the range of effects still consistent with your data. If the CI for a difference includes zero, you cannot rule out 'no effect' — that's what a non-significant result means. Wide CIs scream 'get more data'; narrow ones let you act with confidence.

Base rates are the reality check that saves you from confident nonsense — the classic failure is ignoring the prior probability of an event. Suppose a fraud-detection model is '99% accurate' and flags a Paytm transaction. Should you auto-block it? If only 1 in 10,000 transactions is actually fraud (the base rate), then among 10,000 transactions you'd get roughly 100 false alarms (1% of the ~9,999 honest ones) but only about 1 true fraud caught — so a flag is correct only about 1% of the time. The test's accuracy sounds impressive, yet the rare base rate dominates the math. Whenever you evaluate a test, a screening tool, or a 'signal', always ask: how common is the thing being detected in the first place? Ignore the base rate and you'll chase ghosts.
lessons.example: An e-commerce team tests a new checkout button. Day 2, they peek: B is 'winning' at p = 0.04, so they ship it — and the lift vanishes in production. What happened: with daily peeking, the chance of hitting p < 0.05 by luck alone somewhere in a two-week test is far above 5%. The fix they adopt next time: a power calculation says they need ~25,000 users per arm to detect a 1% lift, so they run the full two weeks and check once. The result comes back +0.4%, 95% CI [-0.3%, +1.1%] — the interval straddles zero, so they correctly conclude 'no proven effect' instead of shipping noise.
lessons.code:
# Base-rate math for a '99% accurate' fraud flag
# Base rate of fraud = 1 in 10,000
N            = 10000
true_fraud   = 1                     # actual frauds
honest       = N - true_fraud        # 9999

caught       = 0.99 * true_fraud     # ~0.99 true positives
false_alarms = 0.01 * honest         # ~99.99 false positives

precision = caught / (caught + false_alarms)
print(round(precision, 3))   # ~0.01  => a flag is right ~1% of the time!
# Lesson: high accuracy + rare event  =>  most alarms are false.
lessons.tryit: Before your next experiment, write a one-paragraph pre-registration: state the metric, the minimum effect worth caring about, the sample size you'll collect, and the single date you'll analyse. After the test, write the result as 'effect, 95% CI [low, high]' and decide based on whether the CI excludes zero — not on whether you can find a p < 0.05.

mistakes.m: Reading p < 0.05 as 'there is a 95% chance B is better than A'.
mistakes.fix: The p-value is the probability of data this extreme *if there were no real difference*. It says nothing directly about the probability that B is better; use effect size and the confidence interval to judge that.

mistakes.m: Peeking at results daily and stopping as soon as significance appears.
mistakes.fix: Repeated peeking inflates false positives well above 5%. Fix the sample size in advance and analyse once, or use a proper sequential/Bayesian method designed for continuous monitoring.

mistakes.m: Judging a detector by its accuracy while ignoring how rare the event is.
mistakes.fix: For rare events, compute precision using the base rate. A 99%-accurate test for a 1-in-10,000 event still produces mostly false alarms.

quiz.q: An A/B test reports p = 0.03. Which interpretation is correct?
opts: There is a 97% probability that version B is better than A
If there were truly no difference, data this extreme (or more) would occur only 3% of the time
Version B improves conversion by exactly 3%
The result is guaranteed to replicate in production
quiz.why: A p-value is computed assuming the null hypothesis (no difference) is true; it measures how surprising the observed data would be under that assumption. It is not the probability that B is better, and it says nothing about effect size or replication.
quiz.approach: Report the effect size and confidence interval alongside p, and never read p as 'probability B wins'.

quiz.q: A team checks their A/B test every day and ships as soon as they first see p < 0.05. Why is this dangerous?
opts: Daily checking is fine and speeds up correct decisions
Repeated peeking inflates the false-positive rate far above 5%
It makes the confidence interval narrower and more accurate
It has no statistical effect at all
quiz.why: Each look is another chance for random noise to cross the 0.05 line. Across many peeks, the probability of a false 'win' somewhere becomes much larger than 5%, so early stopping on the first significant result systematically ships flukes.
quiz.approach: Fix the sample size up front and analyse once, or use a method designed for sequential monitoring.

quiz.q: A fraud model is 99% accurate. Only 1 in 10,000 transactions is actually fraud. A given transaction gets flagged. Roughly how likely is it truly fraud?
opts: About 99%
About 50%
About 1%
Exactly 0%
quiz.why: Per 10,000 transactions there is ~1 real fraud (mostly caught) but ~100 false alarms (1% of the ~9,999 honest ones). So among ~101 flags only ~1 is real — about 1%. The rare base rate overwhelms the high accuracy.
quiz.approach: For rare events, compute precision using the base rate; high accuracy alone does not make an alarm trustworthy.

lessons.title: The p-value Trap: Peeking, Power, and Why Your A/B Test Lies
lessons.concept:
A p-value answers one narrow question: if there were truly NO difference between control and variant, how surprising is data at least this extreme? p < 0.05 means such data would occur under 5% of the time by chance alone. It does NOT mean there is a 95% chance your variant is better, and it says nothing about how big the effect is. This single misreading is the most expensive statistical error on Indian product teams, because a 0.3% lift that is 'significant' on 5 crore users may be commercially meaningless, while a real 4% lift can look 'insignificant' on 800 users.

The deadliest sin is peeking: watching the dashboard daily and stopping the moment p dips below 0.05. Because the p-value random-walks as data accumulates, if you keep peeking you WILL eventually cross 0.05 by pure noise. With continuous monitoring, your true false-positive rate balloons from 5% toward 25-40%. The fix is to fix your sample size in advance via a power calculation, let the test run to completion, and only then read the result — or use methods explicitly built for continuous monitoring (sequential testing / always-valid p-values).

Statistical power is the flip side and is routinely ignored. Power is the probability of DETECTING a real effect of a given size; convention targets 80%. Underpowered tests are worse than useless: they usually miss real wins AND, when they do hit significance, the effect is exaggerated (the 'winner's curse'). Before launching you must fix three things together: the baseline rate, the minimum lift worth caring about (MDE, the minimum detectable effect), and the sample size that gives 80% power at 5% significance. Change one and the others move.
lessons.example: A Flipkart PM tests a new 'Buy Now' button colour. Baseline checkout conversion is 4%. On day 2, with 3,000 users per arm, the dashboard flashes p = 0.04 and the variant is 'up 12%'. The PM wants to ship. But a proper power calculation for detecting a 10% relative lift (4% to 4.4%) at 80% power needs roughly 29,000 users PER ARM. At 3,000 users the test is hopelessly underpowered; that p = 0.04 is almost certainly noise from peeking. Ship it and the 'win' evaporates in production.
lessons.code:
# Sample size for an A/B test (two proportions), Python
from statsmodels.stats.power import NormalIndPower
from statsmodels.stats.proportion import proportion_effectsize

p1 = 0.04          # baseline checkout conversion
p2 = 0.044         # target: +10% relative (the MDE)
effect = proportion_effectsize(p1, p2)

n = NormalIndPower().solve_power(
        effect_size=effect,
        alpha=0.05,      # 5% significance
        power=0.80,      # 80% power
        ratio=1.0,
        alternative='two-sided')
print(round(n))   # ~29,000 users PER ARM

# Lesson: decide n BEFORE the test, then do not peek.
lessons.tryit: Take one metric you actually track (say email signup rate, assume 6%). Decide the smallest lift worth shipping for (e.g. +15% relative, to 6.9%). Plug p1=0.06, p2=0.069 into the code above and compute the sample size per arm. Then estimate how many days your traffic needs to reach it — that is your pre-registered test duration.

mistakes.m: 'p = 0.03 means there's a 97% chance the variant is better.'
mistakes.fix: The p-value is computed ASSUMING no difference exists; it is P(data this extreme | null true), not P(variant better | data). To get the probability a variant wins you need priors and Bayesian methods, or at minimum a confidence interval on the effect size.

mistakes.m: 'Significant means the effect is big and important.'
mistakes.fix: Significance only says the effect is unlikely to be exactly zero. On huge samples trivially tiny effects turn significant. Always report the effect size and its confidence interval, and judge business impact separately from the p-value.

mistakes.m: 'I'll just stop the test as soon as it hits significance to save time.'
mistakes.fix: Optional stopping (peeking) inflates false positives dramatically. Fix the sample size in advance and run to completion, or adopt a sequential/always-valid testing method designed to allow early stopping.

quiz.q: An A/B test on a signup flow returns p = 0.02. Which interpretation is correct?
opts: There is a 98% probability the new flow is better
If the two flows were truly identical, data at least this extreme would occur only 2% of the time
The new flow improves signups by 2%
There is a 2% chance the result is a false positive over the test's lifetime
quiz.why: A p-value is a conditional probability computed UNDER the null hypothesis (flows identical). p = 0.02 means: assuming no real difference, results this extreme (or more) arise 2% of the time. It is not the probability the variant is better, not the effect size, and not a lifetime error rate.
quiz.approach: Always read a p-value as 'how surprising is this data if nothing is going on', then look separately at the effect size and its confidence interval to judge whether the result matters.

quiz.q: Your test needs 29,000 users per arm for 80% power, but you stop at 3,000 because it 'already shows' p = 0.04. What is the biggest risk?
opts: The test is overpowered and wastes traffic
The significance threshold should have been 0.01
The result is likely a false positive from an underpowered, peeked test, and any real effect is exaggerated
Confidence intervals cannot be computed at this sample size
quiz.why: Stopping early at a fraction of the required sample means the test is underpowered and you peeked. Underpowered 'wins' are dominated by noise and, via the winner's curse, overstate the true effect. Reaching significance does not rescue an undersized, optionally-stopped test.
quiz.approach: Fix sample size via a power calculation before launch, run to completion, and resist the urge to ship the moment the dashboard flashes green.

quiz.q: You increase the minimum detectable effect (MDE) from a +5% to a +15% relative lift, keeping alpha and power fixed. What happens to the required sample size?
opts: It increases, because bigger effects need more data
It decreases, because bigger effects are easier to detect
It stays the same, because MDE does not affect sample size
It becomes undefined without knowing the p-value
quiz.why: Larger effects are easier to spot against noise, so detecting a bigger minimum effect needs FEWER samples. Sample size scales roughly with 1/effect-size-squared: caring only about large lifts is cheaper; insisting on detecting tiny lifts is very expensive.
quiz.approach: Choose the smallest lift that is actually worth shipping as your MDE. Chasing detection of trivially small effects can multiply your required sample size and test duration.

lessons.title: Simpson's Paradox: When Every Segment Wins but the Total Loses
lessons.concept:
Simpson's paradox is the unsettling fact that a trend can hold in every subgroup yet reverse when the groups are combined — or the opposite. It happens because of a lurking confounder that is unevenly distributed across the groups and also drives the outcome. The aggregate number is a weighted blend, and if the mix (the weights) differs between the groups being compared, the blend can point the wrong way even when every honest apples-to-apples comparison points the other.

The classic mechanism: imagine a hospital that takes on far more critical patients than its rival. Ward by ward it has a higher survival rate, but because it treats a sicker case-mix, its overall survival looks worse. The 'severity of case' is the confounder; it is correlated both with which hospital you go to and with whether you survive. The paradox is not a maths trick — it is a warning that aggregate rates silently assume comparable mixes, and that assumption is usually false in real data.

The cure is not statistical wizardry but causal thinking: identify the confounder, then compare like with like by stratifying (segmenting) and choosing weights deliberately. Which number is 'right' depends on the question. If you are deciding where to send a NEW patient, the within-ward (stratified) rates are what matter. If you are describing the raw historical outcome of the two hospitals as they actually operated, the aggregate is a fair description. The discipline is to always ask: what is different about the composition of the groups I am comparing?
lessons.example: Consider two loan-collection agents at an NBFC in Pune. Agent A recovers 80% on easy accounts (400 of 500) and 40% on hard accounts (200 of 500) — overall 600/1000 = 60%. Agent B recovers 90% on easy (450 of 500) and 50% on hard (25 of 50) — overall 475/550 = 86%. Agent B beats A overall (86% vs 60%), yet B recovers a HIGHER rate than A in BOTH buckets? No — flip it: give B mostly EASY accounts and A mostly hard ones and A can beat B in each bucket while trailing overall. The mix of easy vs hard accounts assigned to each agent is the confounder; comparing raw recovery rates without controlling for account difficulty would unfairly reward whoever got the easier book.
lessons.code:
# Simpson's paradox in a treatment comparison, Python/pandas
import pandas as pd

df = pd.DataFrame({
  'treatment': ['A','A','B','B'],
  'severity' : ['mild','severe','mild','severe'],
  'recovered': [81, 192, 234, 55],
  'total'    : [87, 263, 270, 80],
})
df['rate'] = df.recovered / df.total

# Within each severity stratum: A beats B
print(df[['treatment','severity','rate']])
#   A mild .93 vs B mild .87  -> A wins
#   A sev  .73 vs B sev  .69  -> A wins

# But aggregate reverses:
agg = df.groupby('treatment').apply(
        lambda g: g.recovered.sum()/g.total.sum())
print(agg)   # A .78  vs  B .83  -> B 'wins' overall
# A treats far more severe cases, dragging its blended rate down.
lessons.tryit: Pull any two-segment metric from your own work (e.g. conversion by device: mobile vs desktop) for two campaigns. Compute each campaign's rate within mobile, within desktop, and overall. Check whether the campaign that wins overall also wins in both segments. If not, find the confounder: which campaign had a heavier share of the easier segment?

mistakes.m: Trusting the single aggregate rate because it uses 'all the data' and must therefore be most reliable.
mistakes.fix: More rows do not fix a biased mix. If subgroups have different compositions, the aggregate blends incomparable populations. Always segment by the plausible confounder before concluding which option is better.

mistakes.m: Assuming the stratified (within-segment) number is always the 'true' answer.
mistakes.fix: It depends on the question. For a forward-looking decision (where to route a new case), stratified rates matter. For describing what actually happened historically, the aggregate is a legitimate summary. State your question first.

mistakes.m: Believing the paradox proves the data is wrong or corrupted.
mistakes.fix: Nothing is wrong with the data. The reversal is a genuine arithmetic consequence of unequal weighting. Treat it as a signal to hunt for the confounder, not as an error to 'clean'.

links.t: Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy: Simpson's Paradox

quiz.q: A drug shows a higher recovery rate than placebo in mild cases AND in severe cases, but a lower rate overall. What must be true?
opts: The data is corrupted and should be discarded
The drug and placebo groups had different mixes of mild vs severe patients
The p-value was computed incorrectly
Recovery rates cannot be compared across severity levels
quiz.why: This is Simpson's paradox: the reversal happens precisely because severity (the confounder) is distributed differently between the drug and placebo groups. If the drug group had far more severe patients, its blended rate is dragged down even while it wins within each stratum.
quiz.approach: When a trend reverses on aggregation, look for a confounder that is unevenly split across the groups and also drives the outcome, then compare like with like.

quiz.q: You are deciding which of two collection agents to assign a NEW hard-to-recover account. Which number should drive the decision?
opts: The agent's overall recovery rate across all accounts
The agent's recovery rate specifically on hard accounts
Whichever agent has more total accounts
The average of the two agents' overall rates
quiz.why: For a forward-looking decision about a hard account, you want the comparison that holds difficulty constant: each agent's recovery rate on hard accounts. The overall rate blends in easy accounts and reflects each agent's historical case-mix, not their skill on the case you are actually assigning.
quiz.approach: Match the statistic to the decision. For routing a specific case, use the stratified rate for that case type; the aggregate answers a different question.

quiz.q: Which condition is REQUIRED for Simpson's paradox to appear?
opts: The sample size must be very small
A confounding variable must be both unevenly distributed across groups and associated with the outcome
The two groups must have exactly equal sizes
The outcome must be a continuous variable
quiz.why: The paradox is driven by a lurking variable that (a) is split unevenly between the compared groups and (b) affects the outcome. Without such a confounder acting as unequal weights, the aggregate cannot reverse the subgroup trends. Sample size, equal group sizes, and variable type are irrelevant to whether it can occur.
quiz.approach: Before comparing aggregate rates, ask what variable could differ in composition across your groups and also move the outcome — that is where the paradox hides.

lessons.title: Base Rates and the Cancer Test That's 90% Wrong
lessons.concept:
The base rate is simply how common something is before you look at any evidence — the prior prevalence. Human intuition, and most dashboards, systematically ignore it, a bias so reliable it has a name: base-rate neglect. When an event is rare, even a highly accurate test produces mostly false alarms, because the flood of true negatives contains a small but overwhelming trickle of false positives that swamps the handful of true positives. This is not a curiosity; it governs fraud detection, disease screening, spam filters, and every 'we flagged 10,000 suspicious transactions' pipeline in Indian fintech.

Bayes' theorem is the machine that combines the base rate with the test's accuracy. Sensitivity is P(test positive | condition present); specificity is P(test negative | condition absent). What you actually want is the reverse — P(condition present | test positive), the positive predictive value — and Bayes flips one into the other. The crucial insight: when prevalence is low, PPV can be shockingly poor even for a test with 99% sensitivity and 99% specificity, because the 1% false-positive rate applied to the huge healthy majority dwarfs the true cases.

The practical habit is to reason in natural frequencies instead of percentages, which makes the trap vanish. Instead of 'sensitivity 90%, specificity 90%, prevalence 1%', say: out of 10,000 people, 100 have the disease and 9,900 do not; the test catches 90 of the sick but also falsely flags 990 of the healthy; so of 1,080 positives only 90 are truly sick — about 8%. Same numbers, but now the answer is obvious. Whenever someone quotes an accuracy figure, immediately ask 'out of how many, and how rare is the thing?'
lessons.example: An Indian bank deploys a fraud model that is 99% sensitive and 99% specific — excellent by any benchmark. But genuine fraud is 0.1% of transactions. Out of 10,00,000 daily transactions, 1,000 are fraud and 9,99,000 are legitimate. The model catches 990 frauds (99% of 1,000) but also false-flags 9,990 legitimate transactions (1% of 9,99,000). So of 10,980 alerts, only 990 are real fraud — a precision of just 9%. The fraud-ops team drowns: nine out of ten blocked cards belong to angry, legitimate customers. The model isn't 'bad' — the base rate makes even 99%/99% produce mostly false alarms.
lessons.code:
# Positive predictive value via Bayes / natural frequencies
def ppv(sensitivity, specificity, prevalence, N=1_000_000):
    sick     = prevalence * N
    healthy  = N - sick
    true_pos = sensitivity * sick
    false_pos= (1 - specificity) * healthy
    return true_pos / (true_pos + false_pos)

print(ppv(0.99, 0.99, 0.001))  # 0.090  -> only 9% of alerts are real
print(ppv(0.99, 0.99, 0.01))   # 0.500  -> raise prevalence, PPV jumps
print(ppv(0.99, 0.999, 0.001)) # 0.497  -> cutting false-positive rate helps most

# Takeaway: for rare events, specificity (low false-positive rate)
# drives precision far more than sensitivity does.
lessons.tryit: Take a screening or alerting system you know (spam filter, KYC risk flag, medical test). Estimate its sensitivity, specificity, and the true base rate of the thing it hunts. Plug them into the ppv function above and compute the precision. If it shocks you, that gap is exactly why the ops queue is full of false alarms.

mistakes.m: 'The test is 99% accurate, so a positive result means 99% chance I have the condition.'
mistakes.fix: Test accuracy (sensitivity/specificity) is not the same as the probability you have the condition given a positive (PPV). For rare conditions, PPV can be far below the accuracy figure. Always fold in the base rate via Bayes.

mistakes.m: Chasing higher sensitivity to improve a rare-event detector.
mistakes.fix: When the base rate is tiny, precision is dominated by the false-positive rate. Improving specificity (fewer false alarms among the vast negative majority) usually raises PPV far more than squeezing out extra sensitivity.

mistakes.m: Reporting only 'we flagged N cases' without the base rate or false-positive volume.
mistakes.fix: A raw count of alerts is meaningless without precision. Always report how many flags were true positives out of total flags, and state the underlying prevalence so stakeholders can judge the alert quality.

quiz.q: A disease affects 1 in 1,000 people. A test is 99% sensitive and 95% specific. Out of 100,000 people, roughly what share of positive results are true positives?
opts: About 99%
About 95%
About 2%
About 50%
quiz.why: Of 100,000 people, 100 are sick and 99,900 healthy. The test finds 99 true positives (99% of 100) but also 4,995 false positives (5% of 99,900). PPV = 99 / (99 + 4,995) is about 1.9%, roughly 2%. The rare base rate means the 5% false-positive rate on the huge healthy group swamps the true cases.
quiz.approach: Convert percentages into natural frequencies out of a fixed population, then read PPV directly as true positives over all positives — the base-rate trap disappears.

quiz.q: For a detector hunting a very rare event, which lever most improves the precision (PPV) of its alerts?
opts: Increasing sensitivity from 95% to 99%
Reducing the false-positive rate (raising specificity)
Collecting a larger sample
Lowering the alert threshold to catch more cases
quiz.why: When the base rate is tiny, false positives come from applying the false-positive rate to an enormous negative majority, and they dominate the alert pool. Cutting that false-positive rate (higher specificity) removes far more junk than squeezing extra sensitivity out of the tiny positive class. Lowering the threshold makes precision worse.
quiz.approach: For rare-event detection, prioritise specificity. A small improvement in the false-positive rate can multiply precision, whereas sensitivity gains barely move it.

quiz.q: Why does the base rate fallacy cause people to overestimate the meaning of a positive test?
opts: Because they confuse P(positive | condition) with P(condition | positive)
Because sensitivity and specificity are always equal
Because larger samples reduce prevalence
Because p-values are not reported
quiz.why: The fallacy is a confusion of conditional directions: people read the test's accuracy, P(positive | condition), as if it were what they want, P(condition | positive). Bayes' theorem shows these differ sharply when prevalence is low, because the prior base rate reshapes the answer.
quiz.approach: Whenever you see an accuracy figure, explicitly ask 'the probability of what, given what?' and use Bayes or natural frequencies to get the direction you actually care about.

lessons.title: Confidence Intervals and Regression: Reading the Uncertainty, Not Just the Point
lessons.concept:
A point estimate without its uncertainty is half a fact. A confidence interval (CI) is the range of plausible values for a quantity, given your sample and a chosen confidence level (usually 95%). Its correct interpretation is procedural and subtle: 'if we repeated this sampling many times and built a 95% CI each time, about 95% of those intervals would contain the true value.' It is NOT 'there is a 95% probability the true value lies in THIS interval' — the true value is fixed, and any single interval either contains it or does not. The width of the CI is the real signal: it shrinks with the square root of sample size, so quadrupling your data only halves your uncertainty.

Regression is the workhorse for asking 'how does Y move with X, holding other things constant?' A coefficient of, say, 1,500 on 'years of experience' in a salary model means: comparing two people who differ by one year of experience but are otherwise identical on the included variables, the model expects a difference of ₹1,500 in salary. Two disciplines matter. First, the coefficient is an association within the model, not a proven cause — it is only 'holding constant' the variables you actually put in, and omitting a relevant one (omitted-variable bias) can flip its sign or size. Second, every coefficient carries its own CI and p-value; a coefficient whose CI comfortably straddles zero is not reliably different from no effect.

R-squared tells you the share of variance in Y explained by the model, but it is widely abused. A high R-squared does not mean the model is correct, causal, or good at prediction on new data — you can inflate it by adding junk variables (which is why adjusted R-squared penalises extra terms). And a low R-squared is not automatically useless: in noisy human behaviour, a model explaining 15% of variance can still reveal a real, actionable relationship. Judge a regression by whether its coefficients are stable, interpretable, and honestly caveated — not by a single headline number.
lessons.example: A Bangalore HR analyst regresses annual CTC on years of experience across 500 employees and gets: CTC = 4,00,000 + 1,50,000 x (years), R-squared = 0.35, with the experience coefficient's 95% CI running from 1,30,000 to 1,70,000. Read it correctly: each extra year is associated with about 1.5 lakh more CTC among otherwise-similar employees in this data, and the interval means the estimate is fairly precise. But because the model omits skill, city, and role, that coefficient is not proof that giving someone another year automatically adds 1.5 lakh — a senior specialist role could be driving both experience and pay. The 0.35 R-squared honestly says experience alone explains just over a third of pay variation; the rest is everything left out.
lessons.code:
# Regression with coefficient confidence intervals, Python/statsmodels
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf
import pandas as pd

# df has columns: ctc, years, city
model = smf.ols('ctc ~ years + C(city)', data=df).fit()
print(model.summary())        # coefficients, p-values, R-squared
print(model.conf_int(0.05))   # 95% CI for each coefficient

# Read the 'years' row:
#   coef      = 150000    -> +1.5 lakh per year, holding city constant
#   [0.025, 0.975] CI     -> plausible range; if it excludes 0, reliable
#   p < 0.05              -> unlikely to be zero by chance
# Adding C(city) controls for city: watch the 'years' coef change
# -> that shift is omitted-variable bias made visible.
lessons.tryit: Fit a simple two-variable regression on data you have (e.g. monthly sales vs marketing spend). Print the coefficient, its 95% CI, and R-squared. Then add one more sensible predictor and re-run. Note how much the original coefficient moves — that movement is a live demonstration of omitted-variable bias, and whether the CI still excludes zero tells you if the effect survives.

mistakes.m: 'There is a 95% probability the true value lies inside this particular confidence interval.'
mistakes.fix: The true value is fixed; the interval is what varies across samples. The correct reading is that 95% of intervals built by this procedure would capture the truth. For a probability statement about this specific interval you need a Bayesian credible interval.

mistakes.m: Reading a regression coefficient as proof of causation.
mistakes.fix: A coefficient is an association conditional on the variables in the model. Omitting a relevant confounder biases it. Only randomisation or careful causal design licenses a causal claim; otherwise report it as an association and list what you did not control for.

mistakes.m: Judging a model by R-squared alone — chasing a high number or dismissing a low one.
mistakes.fix: High R-squared can come from overfitting or irrelevant added variables (use adjusted R-squared, and test on held-out data). A modest R-squared can still capture a real, useful relationship in noisy human data. Look at coefficient stability, CIs, and out-of-sample performance.

quiz.q: A 95% confidence interval for average delivery time is [38, 46] minutes. Which statement is correct?
opts: There is a 95% probability the true mean is between 38 and 46 minutes
95% of delivery times fall between 38 and 46 minutes
If we repeated the sampling many times, about 95% of the intervals built this way would contain the true mean
The true mean is definitely between 38 and 46 minutes
quiz.why: A confidence level describes the long-run behaviour of the PROCEDURE, not the probability for one fixed interval. About 95% of intervals constructed this way capture the true mean. It is not a probability statement about this specific interval, and a CI for the mean says nothing directly about the spread of individual delivery times.
quiz.approach: Interpret confidence intervals as a property of the method across repeated samples; if you truly want 'probability the parameter is in this range', that is a Bayesian credible interval, computed differently.

quiz.q: In a salary regression, adding 'city' as a predictor changes the 'years of experience' coefficient from 1,80,000 to 1,50,000. What does this most likely indicate?
opts: The model is now broken
Omitted-variable bias: city was confounding the experience effect
Experience no longer matters
R-squared must have fallen
quiz.why: When adding a variable shifts an existing coefficient, the new variable was correlated with both the predictor and the outcome — it was confounding the estimate. Controlling for city removes that bias from the experience coefficient. This is omitted-variable bias made visible, not a broken model, and experience still has a substantial effect.
quiz.approach: Watch how coefficients move as you add plausible controls; large shifts warn that your simpler model was confounded and that a causal reading of the original coefficient was unsafe.

quiz.q: A regression predicting customer spend has R-squared = 0.18. What is the best conclusion?
opts: The model is useless and should be discarded
The model explains 18% of the variance in spend, which may still reveal a real, actionable relationship in noisy behaviour
18% of predictions are correct
The coefficients are guaranteed to be statistically significant
quiz.why: R-squared is the share of variance in the outcome explained by the model — here 18%. In noisy human behaviour that can still be genuinely useful and reflect real relationships; it is not a percentage of correct predictions, and it says nothing on its own about whether individual coefficients are significant. A modest R-squared should not be dismissed reflexively.
quiz.approach: Judge a regression by coefficient stability, confidence intervals, and out-of-sample performance, not by R-squared alone; low R-squared with a solid, well-caveated coefficient can still drive good decisions.
